content:
# REAL-TIME PREDICTION OF RHEOLOGY OF OIL-BASED MUD

**Summary**: The thesis investigates the use of artificial intelligence (AI) techniques, specifically Artificial Neural Networks (ANN), Adaptive Neuro-Fuzzy Inference Systems (ANFIS), and Support Vector Machines (SVM), to predict the rheological properties of five types of oil-based mud (OBM). The study aims to provide a real-time monitoring solution by using frequently measured parameters like mud weight and Marsh funnel viscosity as inputs for prediction.

---

## Research Problem

The primary challenge in drilling operations is the low resolution of rheology measurements due to the complexity of lab tests. While the Marsh funnel provides a quick estimation, it does not provide absolute units, leading to a need for automated systems or empirical models that can convert field-measured data (mud weight and Marsh funnel viscosity) into accurate rheological properties in real-time.

## Objectives

- Utilize only high-frequency field measurements (mud weight and Marsh funnel viscosity) as input parameters for real-time monitoring.
- Preprocessing and data analysis for the collected data.
- Building the AI models for the five types of OBM: All-oil Mud, Invert Emulsion, High-overbalance, Flat-rheology, and High-density.
- Develop ANFIS models to predict the rheological properties of oil-based mud (PV, YP, R300, R600, n, and AV).
- Compare developed models against existing literature correlations (e.g., Pitt and Almahdawi) to validate accuracy.
- Provide empirical equations derived from the neural networks for use in automated systems.

## Methods

The study utilizes three primary AI techniques: Artificial Neural Networks (ANN), Adaptive Neuro-Fuzzy Inference Systems (ANFIS), and Support Vector Machines (SVM). Data collection involves gathering real field measurements of mud weight (MWT) and Marsh funnel viscosity (MFV) from various well sites. The data undergoes preprocessing to remove outliers and ensure sufficiency for training. For the ANN models, specific parameters like number of hidden neurons, training functions (e.g., Levenberg-Marquardt), and transfer functions (e.g., tansig) are optimized. Empirical correlations are derived from the trained neural networks to provide a direct mathematical relationship between input measurements (MWT, MFV) and output rheological properties.

## Data and Materials

Data was collected from 56 wells with different sizes across various sections. The dataset included mud weight (MWT) ranging from 56 to 101 pcf, Marsh funnel viscosity (MFV) between 40 and 85 sec, plastic viscosity (PV) from 8 to 47 cP, yield point (YP) from 14 to 28 lb/100 ft2, behavior index (n) from 0.41 to 0.8, R300 between 24 and 74 deg, R600 between 32 and 120 deg, and apparent viscosity (AV) from 16 to 60 cP.

## Key Findings

- The ANN model for plastic viscosity (PV) achieved a correlation coefficient (R) of 0.95 and an Average Absolute Percentage Error (AAPE) of 7.37% during training, with R=0.95 and AAPE=7.97% during testing. ([[2021-alsabaa|Alsabaa, 2021, p. 40]])
- The Yield Point (YP) prediction model achieved an R of 0.91 and an AAPE of 4.77% for the training set, and R=0.90 with AAPE=6.03% for the testing set. ([[2021-alsabaa|Alsabaa, 2021, p. 40]])
- The behavior index (n) model showed high accuracy with an R of 0.93 and a low AAPE of 3.27% in training, and R=0.91 with AAPE=4.81% in testing. ([[2021-alsabaa|Alsabaa, 2021, p. 40]])
- The R600 prediction model showed an R of 0.95 and AAPE of 6.55% in training, and R=0.94 with AAPE=6.95% in testing. ([[2021-alsabaa|Alsabaa, 2021, p. 40]])
- The correlation coefficient between input parameters (MWT) and output properties like PV was high (0.79), while the relationship between MFV and YP was lower (0.3). ([[2021-alsabaa|Alsabaa, 2021, p. 18]])
- The ANFIS model for apparent viscosity (AV) achieved a correlation coefficient of R = 0.96 and AAPE of 5.39% using the MWT and MFV inputs. ([[2021-alsabaa|Alsabaa, 2021, p. 52]])
- The developed AV models outperformed existing literature correlations by Pitt (R=0.76, AAPE=49.94%) and Almahdawi (R=0.75, AAPE=46.08%). ([[2021-alsabaa|Alsabaa, 2021, p. 53]])
- The ANFIS models for all six parameters (PV, YP, R300, R600, n, and AV) showed high accuracy with R > 0.9 and AAPE < 8% during training. ([[2021-alsabaa|Alsabaa, 2021, p. 54]])
- The plastic viscosity (PV) ANFIS model achieved an R of 0.96 and AAPE of 4.59% for the training set, and R = 0.93 with AAPE of 5.31% for the testing set. ([[2021-alsabaa|Alsabaa, 2021, p. 76]])
- The flow behavior index (n) model showed R values of 0.91 and 0.94 with AAPE of 1.68% and 1.66% respectively. ([[2021-alsabaa|Alsabaa, 2021, p. 80]])
- The apparent viscosity (AV) model achieved an R of 0.97 and 0.93 with AAPE of 3.51% and 4.67% for training and testing sets. ([[2021-alsabaa|Alsabaa, 2021, p. 82]])
- The study found that a hidden layer of 32 neurons and the 'tansig' transfer function were optimal for all four models in the high-density mud section. ([[2021-alsabaa|Alsabaa, 2021, p. 74]])
- Correlation analysis showed a strong direct relationship between mud weight and plastic viscosity (R=0.81) and apparent viscosity (R=0.75). ([[2021-alsabaa|Alsabaa, 2021, p. 72]])
- The ANFIS model for AV achieved an R of 0.97 and AAPE of 2.77% when compared to actual values using the training data. ([[2021-alsabaa|Alsabaa, 2021, p. 65]])
- The ANN models achieved high correlation coefficients (R > 0.97) and low average absolute percentage errors (AAPE < 7%) during training for all four rheological properties. ([[2021-alsabaa|Alsabaa, 2021, p. 106]])

## Limitations

Data preprocessing and quality control are noted as major challenges that may require strategies beyond simple engineering sense to ensure high-quality training sets.

## Recommendations and Future Work

The study suggests that these models can be integrated into automated systems with sensors for real-time monitoring of mud rheology, helping engineers manage hole cleaning parameters more effectively.
